1:
The chapter opens with a rebuke wrapped in tenderness, and the witnesses divide their gaze between Samuel's grief and God's forward-looking purpose. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the scene: Samuel had retired to his house in Ramah, resolved to meddle no more in affairs of state and to train up the sons of the prophets, promising himself more satisfaction in young prophets than in young princes. God does not blame him for mourning but for the excess of it, and Henry marks the poignant detail that Samuel had shed no tears over the setting aside of his own sons, yet grieved without measure for Saul; now he must recover himself, since the decree had gone forth and no prayers or tears could reverse it (2 Sam. 12:22–23).

The reformed Gill agrees that the sentence was irreversible, so that Samuel's indulged affection, however natural, was now in vain. The grammatical-historical commentators widen the frame. Keil and Delitzsch observe that though Saul was not deposed but remained king until his death, the consequences of his rejection were speedily brought to light, this secret anointing being the first of them. Ellicott dwells on the pathos that runs through the whole book of Samuel, which never loses an opportunity of telling how men like Samuel and David mourned for the fallen king; and he notes how Bethlehem, from this day forward, gained a strange notoriety in the annals of the world, the cradle of David chosen at last as the birthplace of Christ. The horn of oil he takes, with Stanley, for the consecrated oil preserved in the sanctuary.

Henry draws the same contrast from the vessel itself: Saul had been anointed from a brittle vial, David from a horn, more plentiful and durable, and he recalls the horn of salvation raised up in the house of David (Luke 1:69). The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads mourn as implying that Samuel prayed for Saul's restitution, and insists the oil here is not inauguration but designation, David remaining a subject for years to come. On the great phrase the voices are one. Where the people had provided themselves a king who proved the product of their sinful desires, God says, I have provided me a king — a man after his own heart, to fulfil all his will and serve his glory (Ps. 89:20; Acts 13:22). Israel shall be no loser; let Samuel's joy in the one provided swallow up his grief for the one rejected.
2:
Samuel's startled reply — How can I go? if Saul hear it, he will kill me — draws the witnesses into two connected questions: what to make of the prophet's fear, and how to justify the sacrifice that veils his errand. On the first the reformed Gill is bluntest, finding here a weakness of faith, a fear of man, and a distrust of the divine power, since he that sent Samuel could surely protect him. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry weighs it more gently: the best men are not perfect in their faith, nor is fear wholly cast out on this side heaven; yet he allows the kinder possibility that Samuel was simply seeking direction from heaven how to manage the matter prudently, so as not to expose himself or others more than needful.

The grammatical-historical commentators defend the fear as well grounded. Keil and Delitzsch note that the prophet, who did not generally show himself timid, can only be understood on the supposition that Saul was already given over to the evil spirit of v.14, so that the very worst might be dreaded from his madness; and they observe pointedly that the Lord did not blame Samuel for his anxiety, but simply showed him a way to proceed. Ellicott agrees that the unhappy malady had made rapid progress: the jealous king was changed from the Saul who once honoured Samuel even in his rebellion, and now the aged seer felt that to cross the king's path in any way would forfeit his life.

The second question — the honesty of the cover — the witnesses answer with one voice. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke lays down the principle carefully: the word I am come to sacrifice was strictly true, for Samuel did offer a sacrifice, and indeed could not have gathered Jesse's sons without it; no man in any circumstance should tell a lie, yet no man is obliged in every circumstance to tell the whole truth, provided he tell nothing but the truth and so speak it that the hearer shall not believe a lie by it. Keil and Delitzsch concur that there was no untruth in the command, and Henry adds that as a prophet Samuel might lawfully sacrifice where God appointed, so that he came to sacrifice truly, though he concealed a further end. The Lord's closing pledge, I will show thee what thou shalt do, becomes the chapter's quiet assurance that those who go about God's work in God's way shall be directed step by step.
3:
The verse turns on the twin commands to call Jesse to the sacrifice and to anoint unto me him whom I name, and the witnesses read it as the meeting of a homely feast with the most solemn of consecrations. The reformed Gill takes the invitation at its plainest: every offerer of a peace offering had the right to summon his family and friends to eat the portions that fell to him, a feast before the Lord, and so Jesse and his sons are bidden. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke fills in the custom, that after the blood was poured out and the fat burnt, the worshippers feasted on the flesh of the sacrifice, all save the whole burnt offering being consumed by fire.

The grammatical-historical commentators give the anointing its full weight. Keil and Delitzsch note that Samuel must have communicated the object of his coming to Jesse, since he is the subject who names each son in turn. Ellicott expands into the richest note of the chapter: from very early times anointing marked a man for high office — priests first (Ex. 40:15; Num. 3:3), then peculiarly the high priest (Ex. 29:29), and above all the kings of Israel, so that the favourite title for the monarch became the Lord's anointed. He reckons that David was anointed three times — here by Samuel, then king over Judah at Hebron (2 Sam. 2:4), and finally over all Israel (2 Sam. 5:3) — and that priest, prophet, and king alike were types of the great expected Deliverer ever known as the Messiah, the Christ, the Anointed One.

Here Ellicott records the striking figure of Wordsworth, who read David's three unctions as themselves figurative of the three anointings of Christ: conceived by the Holy Ghost in the Virgin's womb, anointed publicly at his baptism, and set at last at God's right hand as King of the universal Church. The debate, if it can be called one, is only of scope, for the promise I will shew thee what thou shalt do keeps the outcome hidden even from the prophet. The witnesses land together on the same point: the choice is God's alone, disclosed step by step, and the boy about to be marked with the oil stands in the long line of the Lord's anointed that runs on to Christ himself.
4:
Samuel obeys, and the arrival of the aged seer throws the little town into alarm — the elders of the town trembled at his coming, and said, Comest thou peaceably? The witnesses read the tremor as the instinctive fear that the presence of a prophet stirs in a guilty or uncertain conscience. The reformed Gill notes simply that Samuel had done as the Lord spoke, filling his horn and bringing the heifer. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke explains the fright: they knew he was a prophet of the Lord, and dreaded that he had come to denounce some judgment of the Most High against their city.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott paints the scene most vividly. The name and appearance of the old seer were well known in all the coasts of Israel, and here he came suddenly, with the heifer and the long horn of holy oil, upon the quiet and secluded Bethlehem. Why had he come thus among them? Had their remote township been the scene of some unknown and grave crime? What was happening in Israel, that brought Samuel the seer to little Bethlehem? Keil and Delitzsch add that the anxious inquiry presupposes that even under Saul the prophet was in the habit of coming unexpectedly from place to place to reprove and punish wrongdoing, so that his approach naturally set the elders trembling.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads their fear as owing to the strangeness of the visit — Bethlehem being an obscure town, remote from Samuel's home, so that they justly concluded some extraordinary reason brought him — and he glosses their question, comest thou in peace, as our own idiom, is all well? The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the lesson the moment yields: guilt causes fear, and it becomes us to stand in awe of God's messengers and to tremble at his word. He sets the scene beside a greater one, recalling that when the Son of David was born king of the Jews, all Jerusalem was troubled (Matt. 2:3). The witnesses do not divide; together they make the villagers' fright a mirror of the reverence and dread that ought to attend the coming of God's word among men, until the prophet's answer of peace can quiet it.
5:
Samuel's reassuring word — Peaceably: I am come to sacrifice unto the Lord; sanctify yourselves — gathers the witnesses around the theme of preparation for a holy solemnity, and around the peculiar care he shows to Jesse's house. The reformed Gill repeats that Samuel could say this with truth, sacrifice being one real end of his coming, though not the only one, and one he was not obliged to disclose. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the ritual background. Keil and Delitzsch explain that since the ark had been removed from the tabernacle, sacrifice was no longer confined to one sanctuary, so the prophet was accustomed to turn his visits to account by offering, and to build up the people in fellowship with the Lord; sanctify yourselves means to purify by the washings that preceded a sacrificial festival (Ex. 19:10), and come with me to the sacrifice is a compressed phrase for come and take part in it. Ellicott adds that the answer at once reassured the villagers, and that there was nothing unusual in the sanctifying of Jesse and his sons, that being the chief family of the place.

The wesleyan-arminian voices press the inward side. Clarke reads sanctify yourselves as change your clothes, wash your bodies in pure water, and prepare your minds by meditation, reflection, and prayer, that being in the spirit of sacrifice you may offer acceptably. Wesley detects a deliberate difference in the text: the others were only bidden to sanctify themselves, but Jesse and his sons Samuel himself sanctified, which argues a singular care and marks them out, not merely to join in the act of sacrificing, but to be invited to feast upon the remainders.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers it to worship and to Christ. Before solemn ordinances there must be a solemn protestation, and when we come to offer spiritual sacrifices it concerns us, by sequestering ourselves from the world and renewing our dedication to God, to sanctify ourselves; those that come to sacrifice should come peaceably, for religious exercises must not be performed tumultuously. And he lifts the whole to the gospel: when our Lord Jesus came into the world, though men had reason to tremble lest his errand were to condemn, he gave full assurance that he came peaceably, for he came to sacrifice, and brought his offering with him — a body hast thou prepared me (Heb. 10:5). Let us sanctify ourselves, that we may have an interest in his sacrifice.
6:
The procession of Jesse's sons begins, and the first, Eliab, so impresses Samuel that he says within himself, Surely the Lord's anointed is before him. The witnesses treat the moment as a lesson in the fallibility of even a prophet's snap judgement. The grammatical-historical commentators do the close reading. Keil and Delitzsch note that Eliab was tall and handsome, so that Samuel thought, surely the man now standing before Jehovah is the chosen one; but the Lord answered in his spirit, Look not at his form and the height of his stature, for I have rejected him, the eyes being figuratively the outward appearance as against the heart. Ellicott catches the human cause: there was something in the tall and stately presence of the eldest-born that reminded the old man of the splendid youth of Saul, and Eliab seemed in all respects a fit successor to the rejected warrior.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds the irony sharp. It was strange that Samuel, who had been so disappointed in Saul, whose countenance and stature had recommended him as much as any man's, should be so ready to judge another by that very rule. Yet the prophets themselves, when they spoke from their own thoughts rather than under direction, were as liable to mistake as other men, as Nathan once was (2 Sam. 7:3); and God rectified the error by a secret whisper to Samuel's mind. When God would please the people with a king he chose a comely man, but when he would have one after his own heart, he would not be governed by the outside, for men judge by the sight of the eyes, but God does not (Isa. 11:3).

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reads the standing before him as before the Lord, near the altar where God was present in a special manner (Deut. 12:7), and states the diagnosis plainly: Samuel took Eliab for the man he was sent to anoint, wherein he was mistaken, as other prophets were when they hastily spoke their own thoughts before consulting God. The witnesses do not finally divide. They agree that the error was real and instructive: the servant of God, left to his own eyes, reaches for the tallest and the handsomest, and must be corrected by the word that looks deeper, the word v.7 will now spell out.
7:
This is the verse to which the whole scene has been climbing, and the witnesses gather about its single great sentence: the Lord seeth not as man seeth; for man looketh on the outward appearance, but the Lord looketh on the heart. The reformed Gill notes only that the word came by a secret impulse upon Samuel's mind, as if spoken in an articulate voice; but the weight lies in the contrast itself, which the schools unfold with one accord. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set eyes against heart as the outward form against the inward reality, and observe that on this ground the Lord had rejected the stately Eliab, choosing not as man chooses.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke turns the maxim into a caution against usurping the divine prerogative. Man looks on the outward appearance, and it is well he should, and confine his looks to that, for when he pretends to sound the heart he usurps the prerogative of God. Clarke is moved, too, by the manner of the revelation: in what way were these communications made to Samuel? It must have been by direct inspiration into his heart — and what a state of holy familiarity does this argue between God and the prophet, so that he believes Moses himself was not more highly favoured than Samuel.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the doctrine in two strokes. The Lord looks on the heart, first, in that he knows it: we can tell how men look, but he can tell what they are; man looks on the eyes and is pleased with the liveliness that appears in them, but God looks within and sees the very thoughts and intents. And second, in that he judges by it: the good disposition of the heart, its holiness and goodness, recommends us to God and is in his sight of great price (1 Pet. 3:4), not the majesty of the look or the strength of the body. Let us reckon that to be true beauty which is within, Henry urges, and judge of men, so far as we are able, by their minds and not their mien (Isa. 11:3). The voices do not contend; they unite in the confession that God's election passes by the standards of the eye, and that the heart he searches is the ground on which he chooses and the beauty he prizes.
8:
Jesse now calls Abinadab, his second son, and makes him pass before Samuel, only to hear the same verdict: Neither hath the Lord chosen this. The material here is spare, and the witnesses read the moment chiefly as the second beat in a deliberate rhythm of rejection. The reformed Gill records the bare fact that Abinadab, the second son, was presented. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that since Samuel is the one who pronounces upon each son in turn, he must already have made the object of his coming known to Jesse, so that father and prophet together watch the candidates go by while God alone decides.

The force of the verse is best felt by looking back to the fuller comment the witnesses make on the whole review. The grammatical-historical Ellicott pictures an imposing band of gallant youths passing severally before the old seer and being introduced to him, while the divine voice gave no reply to the prophet's mute questioning, warning him only that the external advantages of mere human beauty and strength were no mark of true greatness. Abinadab, whatever his stature and bearing, is dismissed with the others, and the reader begins to grasp that the outward qualifications which had once carried Saul to the throne count for nothing in this choosing.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the theology that makes the repeated no intelligible. Men dispose of their honours and estates to their sons according to seniority and priority of birth, but God does not, for the elder shall serve the younger. Had it been left to Samuel or to Jesse to make the choice, one of these promising elder sons would certainly have been chosen; but God will magnify his sovereignty, both in passing by those that were most likely and in fastening at last on one who seemed least so. So the rejection of Abinadab is not a comment on the young man's worth but a display of the freedom of God, who is not bound by the order of nature or the estimate of men, and who is quietly clearing the ground for the shepherd still absent in the fields. The pattern set here in v.7 governs every son that follows.
9:
Jesse makes a third son, Shammah, pass by, and again the answer falls: Neither hath the Lord chosen this. The witnesses, working with slender material, dwell on the steadiness of the refusal and the patience it must have cost both father and prophet. The reformed Gill identifies the son, Shammah, sometimes called Shimma or Shimeah, the third of Jesse's house. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the narrative forward, marking that Shammah too was made to pass before Samuel and that the same sentence of rejection was pronounced, before Jesse brought his remaining sons in turn.

The very repetition is the point on which the fuller witnesses insist. This is the third of the elder brothers to be presented and passed over, and the drama lies in the accumulation: son after son, each doubtless commended by something in his person or standing, comes and goes without the sign from heaven. Ellicott's picture of the gallant youths filing before the seer, each introduced with a father's natural pride, throws the silence of God into relief — for no reply came back to the prophet's questioning, only the settled refusal to be moved by form or height. The grammatical-historical commentators are content to record the fact without ornament, and that very plainness is instructive: the sacred writer does not pause to describe Shammah's merits, as though to teach that in this choosing they simply did not weigh. What had once carried Saul to the crown — a commanding presence, the bearing of a soldier — is now passed over in silence, three times running.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the pattern its meaning once more: God will magnify his sovereignty in passing by those that were most promising, as much as in fastening upon one less so, and the elder shall serve the younger. Here is the practical comfort the witnesses draw from the scene as a whole: God's choices are not to be predicted from the outward advantages men prize, nor does he honour his servants according to their rank in the family or their standing in the eyes of their neighbours. Shammah's rejection, like his brothers', is the divine hand steadily narrowing the field, refusing every candidate that Samuel or Jesse would have hastened to crown, until only the overlooked youngest remains — and thereby making plain, when the choice does fall, that it was God's own work and not man's, exactly as v.11 will show.
10:
The review reaches its close: Again Jesse made seven of his sons to pass before Samuel. And Samuel said unto Jesse, The Lord hath not chosen these. Here the witnesses turn from the theology of rejection to a quieter puzzle of the household's numbers and its missing member. The reformed Gill is careful with the arithmetic: these are not seven more sons but four more, which with the three already named make seven, and of these four three are elsewhere named — Nathanael, Raddai, and Ozem — while the fourth is nowhere recorded, perhaps because he died young, or was an obscure man of no note, or was born of another mother.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott sharpens the same observation into a small harmony of Scripture. These seven, with David the youngest, make eight sons of Jesse; yet in the genealogy only seven of the family are recorded (1 Chron. 2:13–15), so that one, apparently, of that bright band of youths died young and left no trace in the record. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley reaches the same conclusion, that only seven are named there because one of them was either born of a concubine or died soon after this time.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke adds the staging that the scene requires. This review was plainly not conducted in public; Samuel, Jesse, and the sons must have been together in a private apartment before the general feast upon the sacrifice, for Samuel says they will not sit down until the youngest is fetched. So the solemn passing of the sons happens apart, a family matter watched by the prophet, while the townsmen wait outside for the sacrifice. The small notice of the missing son, on which Gill, Ellicott, and Wesley alike remark, is itself a mark of the record's honesty, for it does not smooth over the gap in the family's numbers but leaves the reader to reckon with a son who left no memorial.

The witnesses do not debate; together they leave us with a house of promising sons, every one of them declined, and the reader poised on the question that must now be asked — whether these are indeed all Jesse's children. The whole file of the eligible has been exhausted, and heaven's choice has settled on none of them, so that the field is emptied for the one whom no one thought to summon; and the very completeness of the rejection is what will presently make the election of the absent shepherd unmistakably the act of God, as v.12 will confirm.
11:
Samuel's baffled question — Are here all thy children? — and Jesse's answer that there remains yet the youngest, keeping the sheep, bring the witnesses to the heart of the chapter's reversal: God's choice of the overlooked. The reformed Gill reasons with the prophet: since none of these was the man, and yet it was to be one of Jesse's sons, Samuel concluded there must be at least one more, and so put his question. The grammatical-historical commentators feel the suspense. Ellicott notes that for a moment the seer is uncertain, the command having been definite that he should anoint the son of Jesse, yet no sign had been given; and he draws the portrait of the absent boy — different from the stalwart band of elder brothers, probably small of stature beside them, with reddish-brown hair and a fair complexion, thought less of by his father and the men of the village, so that Jesse held him of too little account even to present. But God's thoughts are not our thoughts. Keil and Delitzsch render the household detail, that Samuel would not sit round the table until the youngest had come.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley presses Jesse's own estimate: he keepeth sheep, and is therefore, in the father's mind, the most unfit of all his sons for so high an employment; either Jesse did not understand David's wisdom and valour, or judged him disqualified by his mean occupation. And God so ordered it by his providence that David's election might plainly appear to be his own work, and not Samuel's or Jesse's. Wesley reads the very name as prophecy — David signifies beloved, a fit name for so eminent a type of the Beloved Son.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the pastoral fruit. The youngest are commonly the fondlings of a family, yet David seems to have been least set by of all Jesse's sons; many a great genius lies buried in obscurity and contempt, and God often exalts those whom men despise, giving abundant honour to the part that lacked. He was taken from following the ewes to feed Jacob (Ps. 78:71), as Moses from the flock of Jethro, the pastoral life being God's chosen school for kingly power; and Henry hears in it the Son of David, the stone the builders refused, who is yet made the head of the corner (Ps. 118:22). If God will exalt those of low degree, who can hinder?
12:
The shepherd is fetched at last, and the narrator pauses on his looks — ruddy, and withal of a beautiful countenance, and goodly to look to — before the divine word falls: Arise, anoint him: for this is he. The witnesses read the description with a certain care, since the chapter has just taught that the Lord does not choose by the outward appearance. The reformed Gill notes only that David was sent for from the flock and brought in. The grammatical-historical commentators attend to the colouring. Keil and Delitzsch explain ruddy of the reddish hue of the hair, reckoned a mark of beauty in southern lands where the hair is generally black, and add beautiful eyes and good looks, so that, notwithstanding his youth, David was well fitted in form and figure for the office to which the Lord had chosen him, corporeal beauty being one of the outward distinctions of a king. Clarke takes the word more particularly of red or golden hair, ever associated, he thinks, with a delicate skin and a florid complexion.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry weaves the detail into the larger lesson. He observes that no notice is taken of David's clothing, which was no doubt mean and coarse, as shepherds' coats commonly are; he did not change his garments as Joseph did when hurried from prison (Gen. 41:14), yet he had a very honest look, not stately like Saul's, but sweet and lovely — a clear complexion, a good eye, and features that were charming. Though his employment exposed his face to the sun and wind, nature kept its own, and the sweetness of his aspect gave manifest indication of an amiable temper; perhaps his modest blush, when he was brought so unexpectedly before the seer, made him the handsomer.

The witnesses do not divide, and they are careful not to contradict the theology of v.7. David's beauty is real and even fitting in a future king, but it is not the ground of his election; the Lord who had refused the tall Eliab now says of the ruddy youth, this is he, not because the eye approves him but because the heart of God has chosen him. The comeliness is a gift that accompanies the calling, not the reason for it, and heaven's word, not the shepherd's face, settles the matter.
13:
The scene reaches its consecration: Samuel took the horn of oil, and anointed him in the midst of his brethren: and the Spirit of the Lord came upon David from that day forward. The witnesses gather about two things — the silence that surrounds the act, and the descent of the Spirit that follows it. The reformed Gill marks only that Samuel drew the horn he had brought by God's direction. The grammatical-historical commentators dwell on the reticence. Keil and Delitzsch note that nothing is recorded of any word of Samuel explaining the anointing's meaning. Ellicott presses this: the history relates the bare fact, and it seems most probable that the seer kept silence about the boy's high destiny, anointing him rather as one chosen to be his assistant and pupil, so that from this time much of David's life was spent in Samuel's company, trained by the wise seer in poetry and music and in the early lessons of wisdom that fitted him to fill so nobly the great place for which he was marked out.

On the Spirit the witnesses speak with fuller voice. Ellicott identifies this Spirit of the Lord as the Holy Ghost, formally opposed to the evil spirits that are persons of a spiritual subsistence, citing Bishop Pearson and the lying spirit sent against Ahab (1 Kings 22:22); its effect was that the shepherd boy grew into a hero, a statesman, a scholar, and a wise, far-sighted king. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke lists the graces conferred — wisdom, prudence, counsel, courage, liberality, and magnanimity — and Wesley adds that David's brethren saw the unction but did not understand that he was anointed to the kingdom, being told only that he was set apart for some great service.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry binds it together. The anointing signified both a divine designation to the government, to come in due time after Saul's death, and a communication of gifts and graces to fit him for it, making him a type of the Messiah, the Anointed One, who received the Spirit without measure. And Henry draws the believer's comfort: the best evidence of our being predestinated to the kingdom of glory is our being sealed with the Spirit of promise and finding a work of grace in our own hearts. This same Spirit made David the sweet psalmist of Israel (2 Sam. 23:1); and his work done, Samuel went up to Ramah in peace, his eyes having seen the sceptre brought into the tribe of Judah.
14:
With the anointing of David the narrative turns abruptly to Saul: the Spirit of the Lord departed from Saul, and an evil spirit from the Lord troubled him. The witnesses read the two clauses as one judgement — the withdrawal of good and the incoming of evil — and divide only over the nature of the affliction. The reformed Gill takes the departed Spirit as the spirit of prophecy that had once come on Saul, and the spirit of wisdom and prudence for government, and of fortitude and courage, all now removed. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry frames the whole chapter here as Saul falling and David rising: having forsaken God, Saul is in righteous judgement forsaken by the assistances of the good Spirit, and loses all his good qualities, for when the Spirit departs, all good goes (2 Sam. 7:15). Those who drive away the good Spirit become of course the prey of the evil; if God and his grace do not rule us, sin and Satan will have possession of us. So Saul grew fretful, peevish, timorous, and suspicious, and, as Josephus says, a very demoniac by fits.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch insist this was no mere melancholy but a higher evil power that took possession of the king, depriving him of peace and at times driving him to madness. The wesleyan-arminian commentators weigh the balance of natural and supernatural. Clarke judges the malady of a mixed kind, natural and diabolical, too much of nature in it to be all spiritual and too much of supernatural to be all natural; God took from Saul the gifts he had and gave them to David, for what God fills not, the devil will — and Clarke even entertains Scheuchzer's theory that the morbid tension of the king's fibres, relaxed by music, might restore the free circulation of the animal spirits. Wesley reads from the Lord as by God's permission, Saul being delivered up to be buffeted of Satan, stirred to envy, rage, fear, and despair.

The chief division, then, is over the register of the trouble — whether to weight it toward a real demonic oppression (Keil and Delitzsch), a diabolic possession working through the body (Henry), or a mingled madness in which the devil exploited a natural melancholy (Clarke, Wesley) — and over the sense of from the Lord, which all resolve into divine permission rather than authorship of evil. Beneath the dispute the witnesses are one: the same rejection that emptied Saul of the Spirit opened him to the tormentor, and the vacancy left by grace is never long unfilled.
15:
Saul's attendants name his disorder to his face — Behold now, an evil spirit from God troubleth thee — and the witnesses read the verse as the court's frank diagnosis and the first step toward the remedy of music. The reformed Gill takes the speakers for Saul's courtiers, who observed him acting in a frantic, melancholy, timorous, and unsteady manner, or perhaps his physicians called in to help. The grammatical-historical commentators develop the counsel that follows. Keil and Delitzsch note that the officers, perceiving the king's ailment, advised that the evil spirit be charmed away by instrumental music, and they gather the ancient testimony to music's power over the mind: the wise men of Greece recommended it to calm the passions, heal mental disease, and check even tumults among the people, as Pythagoras is said to have done. Ellicott describes the malady more fully as an incurable melancholy that at times blazed into fits of uncontrollable jealous anger, working on Saul's nervous, excitable temperament, on which sacred music had already shown its influence (1 Sam. 10:10; 1 Sam. 19:23).

Ellicott traces the sad progress of this insanity: the solemn declaration through Samuel that the kingdom was torn from him weighed on Saul's mind, so that he grew gloomy and suspicious of his dearest friends, sought their lives, and at length commanded terrible massacres such as that of the priests at Nob (1 Sam. 22:17–19). As the life advanced, the nobler traits grew fainter and the evil more obvious, a species of insanity fatal alike to the victim and to the kingdom.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry credits the servants with honesty and courage for telling the king plainly what his distemper was, an evil spirit and not mere chance troubling him; yet he laments the direction of their care. How much better friends they would have been, since the evil spirit was from the Lord, had they advised Saul to make his peace with God by true repentance, and sent for Samuel to pray with him and intercede for him; then might he have had not only present relief but the return of the good Spirit. The witnesses do not divide over the diagnosis, only over its cure: all agree the trouble is real and from God's hand in judgement, and the deeper question, which the coming counsel of music does not touch, is whether the king will seek the God who alone can restore him.
16:
The servants propose their remedy — seek out a man who is a cunning player on a harp, that when the evil spirit is upon Saul he may play and the king be well. The witnesses read the counsel with sympathy but also with a careful qualification about what music can and cannot do. The reformed Gill glosses the phrase thy servants which are before thee of the inferior officers or waiters at court, ready to seek such a player. The grammatical-historical commentators rehearse again the ancient conviction of music's power. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott alike cite the instances gathered from antiquity, how Pythagoras would steep his mind in divine power by having the harp played before he slept, and how the physician Aesculapius was said to restore sick souls with music.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley states the crucial limit. Music cannot directly work upon an evil spirit to drive him away; but the devil, it seems, had not possession of Saul, and only made use of the passions of his mind and the humours of his body to molest him. Since music has a mighty power to qualify and sweeten these, and to make a man sedate and cheerful, it is no wonder the tormentor had less power over Saul when his mind was more composed — as, Wesley observes, the devil had less power over lunatics in the moon's decrease than in its increase (Matt. 17:15). And since music prepared the Lord's prophets to receive the good Spirit (2 Kings 3:15), why might it not dispose Saul to resist the evil one?

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry weighs the servants' project shrewdly. Their aim was to make the king merry and so cure him, and Henry warns that many a convinced and startled conscience has been ruined by just such methods, which drown all care of the soul in the delights of sense. Yet he grants that they did not amiss to send for music as a help to cheer the spirits, had they but joined with it a prophet to give good counsel; and it was well, he adds, that they did not send for a witch or a diviner to charm the evil spirit away by enchantment. So the witnesses converge: the harp is a lawful and even useful means, working on body and mind to shut the passages by which the tempter has access, but it is no cure for the soul, and the good it promises is real only within its bounds.
17:
Saul closes with the counsel at once: Provide me now a man that can play well, and bring him to me. The material on this brief verse is slight — the reformed Gill notes only that Saul approved and was pleased with the advice his servants gave — yet the witnesses' larger reading of the passage lets the small sentence carry real weight. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, treating the command together with the counsel before it, observe that Saul, taking his officers' advice, ordered them to seek out a good player upon a stringed instrument, and that this opened the door for one of the young men to speak of the son of Jesse.

The significance the witnesses find is providential rather than verbal. Saul, emptied of the Spirit and harried by the tormentor, reaches for a remedy — and in reaching for it unknowingly summons to his own court the very man whom the Lord has anointed to succeed him. The king who had been told by Samuel of a neighbour better than he, to whom the kingdom should be given, now gives the order that will bring that neighbour to stand before him and to soothe him with his harp. The irony is unmistakable, and the witnesses see in it the hand of a providence that works its purposes through the ordinary decisions of men.

This is the lesson the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws from the whole movement: those whom God designs for any service, his providence will concur with his grace to prepare and qualify, and here David is brought to court and into the sight of the nation without any contrivance of his own or his friends. Saul's simple wish for a skilful musician is the instrument by which heaven advances its plan. The verse thus stands as a hinge — the king's need, honestly felt and plainly spoken, becomes the appointed occasion for the rise of David; and the reader, who knows what Saul does not, watches the rejected king command the fetching of his own successor, all under a providence that neither of them can see.
18:
One of the young men answers with a portrait of David so full that it reads like a herald's commendation: cunning in playing, a mighty valiant man, a man of war, prudent in matters, a comely person, and the Lord is with him. The witnesses attend to who spoke, how such a character could already be true, and what crowns it. The reformed Gill first clears away a legend: the Jews say the speaker was Doeg the Edomite, moved by envy to expose David to danger, but this is unlikely; rather it was a friend who wished to promote him, directed by the overruling providence of God. The grammatical-historical Ellicott notes that the word here for the speaker (the young men) differs from that used of the officers just before, laying stress on his youth; probably a contemporary of David, very likely a fellow-pupil trained with him in Samuel's prophetic school at Naioth in Ramah, and so able to speak of the shepherd in such detail.

The description as a man of war raises a question, since the combat with Goliath still lay ahead. Keil and Delitzsch answer that it does not presuppose experience in battle but is fully explained by David's own account of his conflicts with the lion and the bear (1 Sam. 17:34–35), which had already displayed his courage and strength. Ellicott adds that Bethlehem was a garrison-post of the Philistines, so that David had most likely been engaged in repelling their marauding raids as well (2 Sam. 23:14; 1 Chron. 11:16). The wesleyan-arminian Wesley bids us not wonder that a poor shepherd was so suddenly of such repute, for these were the effects of that Spirit of the Lord which he received when he was anointed, the crowning clause — the Lord is with him — meaning that God directs and prospers all his undertakings.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes that the point of the whole. Though David, after his anointing, returned to his country business and bore no visible marks of the oil, so carefully did he keep the secret, the workings of the Spirit could not be hid, but made him shine in obscurity, so that his neighbours wondered at the sudden improvement of his mind; even in his shepherd's garb he had become an oracle and a champion, and his fame reached the court, for Saul was inquisitive after such young men (1 Sam. 14:52). When the Spirit of God comes upon a man, Henry says, he will make his face to shine. The witnesses do not finally divide over the speaker's identity or David's untried valour; together they see the anointing of v.13 already bearing its fruit, the hidden gift betraying itself and drawing the notice that providence will use.
19:
The summons goes out: Wherefore Saul sent messengers unto Jesse, and said, Send me David thy son, which is with the sheep. The witnesses read the verse as the moment providence draws David out of obscurity, and they mark the courtesy and the detail of the call. The reformed Gill notes that Saul sent to Jesse for David rather than simply taking him, though Samuel had warned that kings would seize men's sons for their service. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record that Saul sent to ask Jesse for his son, and that the request would presently be met with a present, a token of the simple customs of Israel and of Saul's court at that time.

What the witnesses find most telling is the phrase which is with the sheep. David, though anointed and now famed at court for his gifts, is still at his humble work in the fields, keeping his father's flock; the future king has not pushed himself forward, but waits in his calling until he is sent for. The anointing had not lifted him out of his shepherd's duty, nor made him restless for advancement; he is found where duty had left him, among the ewes, exactly as he had been when Samuel first called for him.

The point the passage yields is the one the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses upon the whole episode: David is brought to court, and into the way of preferment, without any contrivance of his own or his friends, by a providence that concurs with grace to prepare God's servants for the service he designs them. The king's message reaches into the sheepfold and calls the anointed youth toward the throne he does not yet claim, and it does so under a divine ordering that neither Saul who sends nor Jesse who will obey can fully perceive. The witnesses do not debate here; they simply watch the shepherd summoned, and see in the quiet obedience of a boy still with the sheep the humility that fits him for what is coming, the same providence that governs v.20 gently drawing him up from the fold.
20:
Jesse's response is an act of homage as much as of obedience: Jesse took an ass laden with bread, and a bottle of wine, and a kid, and sent them by David his son unto Saul. The witnesses read the gift in the light of Eastern custom and, in one case, of a disputed chronology. The reformed Gill dwells on the load, an ass laden with as much bread as it could carry, and a skin or bottle of wine larger than our vessels. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the meaning of the present. Keil and Delitzsch observe that these simple gifts show how homely were the customs of Israel and of Saul's court, and Ellicott adds that it was, and is, customary in the East to acknowledge subjection with a present, so that Jesse, the sheik of Bethlehem, would be expected to own his sovereign with some token of homage when sending his son to court, gifts whose nature betrays how primitive the manners of the people still were (compare the bread and wine of 1 Sam. 10:3).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads Jesse's willingness through the lens of providence. He sent his son readily, and a present with him; probably Jesse, who knew what David was designed for, was aware that providence was fitting him for it, and so would not force events by sending him to court uncalled, yet followed providence cheerfully when he saw it plainly opening the way. Henry mentions the alternative guess, that Jesse feared Saul had heard of the anointing and sent the present to pacify him, but he prefers the reading of glad obedience.

Here the wesleyan-arminian Clarke introduces the one real controversy, the chronological theory of Bishop Warburton, who held that the latter part of the chapter is told out of its order. On Warburton's scheme David had already vanquished the Philistine, become the people's favourite, excited Saul's jealousy, and retired; only then, when Saul was seized with his disorder, did the servants delicately recommend the son of Jesse, whose prudence and proven valour they could commend without exciting the king's fear. Clarke reports the argument as having considerable plausibility, that the anticipation makes the whole history plainer, though he grants the intelligent reader may still have his doubts. The witnesses, then, agree on the meaning of the gift and divide only over its date: whether these events follow the combat with Goliath, as Warburton urges, or stand where the text places them. Wesley simply reminds us that so modest a present was fully agreeable to the usage of those times and to Jesse's mean condition in the world.
21:
And David came to Saul, and stood before him: and he loved him greatly; and he became his armourbearer. The witnesses read the verse as the strange, providential meeting of the rejected king and his anointed successor, and they linger over Saul's affection and the office he confers. The reformed Gill notes that David stood before Saul as a servant, ministering to him for the purpose for which he was sent. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch observe that as David served him with his playing, Saul took a great liking to him and made him his armour-bearer, that is, his adjutant, as a mark of favour; and they draw the wonder of it — David came to Saul's court as his benefactor, without Saul having any suspicion of the young man's divine election to be king of Israel. This guidance, they say, was providential.

The same commentators guard the sequel against a seeming contradiction. Ellicott judges that David was armour-bearer only for a very short time, returning afterward to Samuel, whose pupil and friend he was; for Saul, from his later question, Whose son is this youth? (1 Sam. 17:55), appears to have forgotten all about him. There is no note of time to tell how long a period elapsed before the combat with the Philistine, and the king's malady, making rapid progress, had likely obscured his once powerful mind, so that his memory of the harper failed him.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley draws a gentler observation from Saul's love. That he loved David greatly shows there was something good in Saul still: he had not lost all, though he had lost the kingdom. The office of armour-bearer was conferred, though we do not read that David ever exercised it, for it seems he had gone back to his father before the war that follows, and abode with him some considerable time. The witnesses do not so much debate as harmonise: the difficulty is only apparent, resolved by David's return home and by Saul's failing memory, and beneath it lies the marvel on which they all agree — that the Lord's anointed was drawn into the very household of the man he would replace, cherished by him, and set near the throne, all unrecognised, by a providence the king could not read.
22:
Saul's request to Jesse — Let David, I pray thee, stand before me; for he hath found favour in my sight — is brief, and the witnesses read it for the king's courtesy and for the establishing of David at court. The reformed Gill marks the condescension in it: Saul shows great respect to Jesse in not detaining his son without leave, and asks it as a favour that David might continue in his service. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the phrase let David stand before me as let him remain in my service, Saul sending word to Jesse because David had found favour in his sight, so that the young man is now fixed, by the king's own desire, in the place providence had brought him to.

The courtesy is the more remarkable against Samuel's earlier warning. When Israel had demanded a king, Samuel had told them the manner of the king would be to take their sons for his own service whether they willed it or not (1 Sam. 8:11); yet here Saul, contrary to that arbitrary manner, sues to a Bethlehemite father for the keeping of his son. The witnesses see in this both a genuine kindness in Saul and a providential gentleness in the way David is drawn to the throne — not seized, but requested; not thrust forward, but welcomed on the ground of a favour honestly won.

The weight of the verse, for the witnesses, lies in that word favour. David had found favour in the sight of the very king whom he was destined to succeed, and by that favour he is settled at court, near the seat of power, learning its ways, while the secret of his anointing remains hid. The rejected Saul, harried by the evil spirit, cannot know that in loving and retaining this servant he is nurturing his own successor; and Jesse, granting his son to the king's request, follows the same providence that has governed the whole chapter. The witnesses do not divide; they simply mark how quietly, through a king's kindness and a father's consent, the Lord's anointed is brought to stand continually before the throne, and how the favour David wins with his harp in v.23 secures his place there.
23:
The chapter ends where the servants' counsel promised: when the evil spirit from God was upon Saul, David took an harp, and played with his hand: so Saul was refreshed, and was well, and the evil spirit departed from him. The witnesses read the close for the power of the music, the mystery of the affliction, and the type of a greater Deliverer. The reformed Gill offers a striking alternative reading, that of Abarbanel, who took the spirit here — the word evil being absent from the Hebrew text — not for a demon but for the Spirit of God, stirring in Saul thoughts of divine things and of his rejection, and so filling him with grief and melancholy. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke weighs the same textual point, noting that evil stands in the Vulgate, Septuagint, Targum, and Syriac and in several manuscripts, and concludes that Saul's malady was of a mixed kind, natural and diabolical at once. Wesley marks that the spirit departed only for a season, the success being partly natural and partly supernatural, God using it to bring David into the king's favour.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the mechanism the others imply: David's music was Saul's physic, for music has a natural tendency to compose and exhilarate a disturbed mind, as Elisha used it (2 Kings 3:15); not that it charmed the evil spirit, but it made Saul's spirit sedate and allayed the tumults by which the devil had advantage against him. Henry borrows Bochart's image: the sunbeams cannot be cut with a sword, quenched with water, or blown out with wind, but by closing the shutters they may be kept from the room; so music cannot work upon the devil, yet it may shut up the passages by which he gains access to the mind.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott lifts the scene highest, gathering the witness of Luther, who called music one of the fairest gifts of God, to which Satan is a bitter enemy, and of Basil, that psalmody silences the wave and repels demons; and, with F. D. Maurice, he hears in the harp more than a palliative — an assurance of a secret inward harmony, a prophecy that there is a Deliverer who can take the vulture from the heart and unbind the sufferer from the rock. So the witnesses close in one confession: the boy minstrel who quiets the tormented king is himself the shadow of the Son of David, and the true and lasting cure of a wounded conscience is not the harp but the sacrifice and the Spirit to which David's playing only points.
